sanskrit:
सुखं त्विदानीं त्रिविधं श्रृणु मे भरतर्षभ।
अभ्यासाद्रमते यत्र दुःखान्तं च निगच्छति॥ ३६॥
यत्तदग्रे विषमिव परिणामेऽमृतोपमम्।
तत्सुखं सात्त्विकं प्रोक्तमात्मबुद्धिप्रसादजम्॥ ३७॥
transliteration:
sukhaṁ tv idānīṁ tri-vidhaṁ śṛṇu me bharatarṣabha |
abhyāsād ramate yatra duḥkhāntaṁ ca nigacchati || 36 ||
yat tad agre viṣam iva pariṇāme ’mṛtopamam |
tat sukhaṁ sāttvikaṁ proktam ātma-buddhi-prasāda-jam || 37 ||
literal_meaning: Happiness—now—threefold—hear—from Me—O bull of the Bhāratas—by practice—one delights—where—distress’s end—and—one reaches—which—that—in the beginning—like poison—in the result—like nectar—that—happiness—in the mode of goodness—is declared—of the self—of the intellect—born of clarity.
contextual_meaning: Krishna distinguishes genuine, growth-oriented happiness (sāttvika) from fleeting pleasure: it initially feels bitter or effortful—like swallowing medicine—but matures into deep, self-validating fulfillment that ends existential distress.
psychological_state: Readiness to trade short-term comfort for long-term meaning; tolerance of therapeutic discomfort.
trigger_scenario: Deciding to enter therapy, start meditation, or begin recovery work—knowing the first weeks will feel worse before life improves.
anchor_text: Choose the happiness that starts bitter but ends sweet.
